findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You've got the problem buried in your startup information section on slide 2, but investors need it to hit them first and hard as its own dedicated slide right after your cover. Create a visual problem slide that leads with one stark stat—like "70% of foreign investors abandon their UAE property search within 2 weeks due to delayed agent responses"—then show the three core pain points (fragmentation, slow responses, lack of data-driven insights) with a quick visual or icon for each, so investors immediately feel the urgency before you pitch the solution.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You've got your problem statement and business model laid out clearly, but you're missing a dedicated slide that shows investors exactly how MIRA works from a user's perspective. Create a single "How It Works" or "Solution in Action" slide that walks through one complete customer journey—for example, a foreign investor discovering MIRA on WhatsApp, getting AI-matched to three off-plan properties with AR floor plans, seeing ROI forecasts, and closing the deal with automated docs—with simple visuals or screenshots showing each step. This makes your solution tangible instead of abstract, and gives investors confidence that the platform actually delivers what you're claiming.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You've got AED 800K in revenue from six months of operation, which is impressive, but investors need to see the full financial picture to understand if this is sustainable. Create a one-page financials slide showing your revenue breakdown by stream (commission share, developer partnerships, SaaS, etc.), your monthly burn rate, and a simple 24-month projection—for example: "Month 1-6: AED 800K from commissions + developer fees; Projected Month 7-12: AED 1.2M as SaaS licensing scales; Burn: AED 150K/month declining to AED 80K by month 12 as revenue ramps." This gives

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing the UAE real estate market size, growth rate, and your specific addressable market within it—right now investors see you mention a $280B global commission pool but have no sense of how big the Dubai/UAE opportunity is, what percentage you're targeting, or how you'll capture it compared to competitors like Property Finder and Bayut who already dominate locally. Include a simple TAM/SAM/SOM breakdown for UAE and your expansion markets (Saudi, UK) with the actual numbers and your year-one revenue target mapped to it.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that maps MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors operating in UAE/MENA real estate—name Property Finder, Bayut, and Huspy directly, then create a simple comparison table showing how MIRA differs on key dimensions like AI-driven lead qualification, commission structure, geographic coverage, and immersive tech (AR/VR). For example: "Property Finder: Portal-based, agent-dependent listings | MIRA: AI agent, 24/7 automation, off-plan focus" so investors immediately see why MIRA isn't just another listing site.

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: You're listing "AR/VR + NLP + CRM" as your tech stack, but investors will ask what's actually proprietary here—these are all commodity tools. Add one slide showing your specific architectural advantage: for example, explain how your NLP model is fine-tuned on UAE real estate data to catch market-specific pricing signals that generic LLMs miss, or detail how your AR visualization pipeline reduces property viewing time by X% compared to competitors. Without this, you're describing features, not defensibility.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on your proprietary moat that answers three specific questions: Is your AI model trained on MIRA-exclusive real estate datasets (property histories, transaction patterns, buyer behavior) that competitors can't easily replicate? Do you have exclusive partnerships or data-sharing agreements with DLD, developers, or mortgage providers that create switching costs for agents and buyers? And what's your IP strategy — are you filing patents on your emotional intelligence engine or AR visualization approach? For example, you could show "Proprietary dataset: 50,000+ UAE transaction records + behavioral data from 12,000+ agents = 18-month training advantage over Property Finder" or "Exclusive DLD integration agreement (2-

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Technology slide that states exactly where MIRA stands: "Platform Status: Live with 15+ active agents in UAE pilot (Q4 2024)" or whatever your actual stage is—beta, live, scaling. Right now you're claiming "strong traction and revenue" but investors can't tell if that's from a fully functional product or a limited proof-of-concept, which kills credibility. Be specific about user count, transaction volume, or deployment scope so the 70% conversion rate and revenue claims land as real validation, not aspirational.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that walks through a real customer interaction step-by-step—for example, "Day 1: Buyer logs in and inputs investment criteria → AI generates 3 personalized property matches with AR walkthroughs → Buyer schedules virtual tour → Agent follows up with financing options → Deal closes in 14 days." This concrete narrative shows investors exactly how your 70% conversion rate and 60% budget increase actually happen, rather than just stating the metrics.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated section on your tech stack that explicitly states whether your AI model is proprietary or built on third-party LLMs (like OpenAI, Anthropic), and what IP you own versus license. For example: "MIRA's core NLP engine is built on [OpenAI GPT-4 / custom fine-tuned model], with proprietary layers for real estate domain adaptation and emotional intelligence scoring. We hold patents on [specific methodology], and our AR visualization pipeline is custom-built in-house." This directly addresses investor concern about your defensibility and removes the ambiguity that's currently flagged as a competitive risk.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that spells out exactly how you protect HNI and institutional client data—name the specific standards you meet (ISO 27001, SOC 2, DFSA compliance for UAE), describe your encryption approach (end-to-end for property data, at-rest standards), and clarify who has access to sensitive financial information in your AI workflows. For example: "All client financial data and property valuations are encrypted with AES-256 at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit; MIRA's AI models process anonymized datasets only, with raw client data stored in segregated, DFSA-compliant vaults accessible only to authorized compliance officers."

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your actual unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for each segment, show how much you spend to acquire one customer (CAC), what lifetime value they generate (LTV), and your payback period—for example, "B2B Developer CAC: AED 5K, LTV: AED 180K, 12-month payback" and "B2C Buyer CAC: AED 800, LTV: AED 12K, 18-month payback." This tells investors immediately whether your unit economics work and which customer type is actually driving your profitability.

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts (the real proof of paying customers) versus one-time deals or pilot fees, then show the trend—for example, "AED500K recurring from 12 signed developer partnerships averaging AED42K annually, plus AED300K from initial implementation fees." This tells investors you have predictable, repeatable revenue, not just one-off wins.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions investors will ask, not answering them with your actual strategy. Pick one market (Saudi Arabia or UK) and spell out exactly how you'll win there: for example, "We're targeting Saudi developers through direct partnerships with the top 5 real estate firms—we've already had initial conversations with Dar Al-Baraka and Emaar—while running Arabic-language paid search to reach end-buyer investors, undercutting Property Finder's pricing by 30%." This shows you've thought through the specific channels, competitors, and economics, not just that you plan to expand.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple growth chart to your Traction slide showing your revenue progression month-by-month for the past 6-12 months with actual numbers labeled—for example, "Jan: AED 45K → Feb: AED 62K → Mar: AED 89K" alongside the percentage growth rate (e.g., "38% MoM growth"). This gives investors concrete proof that your AED 800K claim is real and accelerating, not a one-time spike.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide or section showing 2-3 specific customer wins with their names, logos, and one concrete metric each—for example, "Emaar Properties: 40% faster property inquiry response time" or "Damac Developer: 250+ qualified leads generated in Q3 2024." This transforms your AED800K revenue claim from a number into proof, and gives investors confidence that real businesses are already betting on MIRA.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong expertise across fundraising, strategy, and governance, but you're missing someone who owns the day-to-day execution of your core platform — right now it reads like a team of advisors rather than operators building the product. Add a Chief Technology Officer or Chief Operating Officer to your core leadership (separate from your advisor roster) with a specific track record shipping a platform or marketplace, so investors see who's actually building Deal360 and the Hub-and-Spoke model every day.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your team slide shows expertise areas but no proof that your founders have actually built or scaled anything—investors need to know why you're qualified to run this ecosystem play specifically. Add a brief line under each founder's name showing either a prior startup they built (with outcome), a relevant company they scaled, or specific domain wins—for example, "Shirin: Built HR tech platform acquired by [company] in 2020" or "Hazem: Led brand growth at [regional unicorn], 3M+ users." This gives investors confidence you know what you're asking founders to do.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong expertise on your team, but investors need to see who's backing you at the board or advisor level—people with proven track records who've scaled companies or led major funds themselves. Add a dedicated "Board & Advisors" section that names 2-3 heavyweight advisors with their past wins (e.g., "Former VP at [Fund Name] managing $500M+", "Founded [Company] acquired for $X", "Led [Initiative] across 15 countries"), so investors immediately understand who's validating your vision and opening doors.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to your Team slide stating something like "Founding team holds X% equity with Y-year vesting schedules" — this reassures investors that you're aligned long-term and prevents them from asking about it later, which always feels defensive. For example, if you're the CEO with two co-founders, you might say "Founding team: 60% equity pool, 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" right below your names on Slide 10.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated compliance and regulatory section to your deck that spells out exactly which UAE real estate licenses MIRA holds or is pursuing (e.g., RERA registration, brokerage license, data protection compliance), and clarify whether MIRA operates as a technology platform supporting licensed agents or as a licensed brokerage itself—investors will assume regulatory risk is unmanaged if you don't address this head-on, especially since you're handling transactions worth AED 50M+.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Legal & Compliance" slide that explicitly addresses your regulatory standing in the UAE and any other markets you're operating in—specifically: which real estate and AI brokerage licenses you hold (or are pending), how you've structured MIRA to comply with DLD regulations and data privacy laws, and whether you've faced or resolved any regulatory challenges to date. For example: "MIRA operates under UAE Real Estate Regulatory Authority License #[X], complies with DFSA data handling requirements for international investors, and has received legal clearance from [firm] confirming AI agent classification under current UAE brokerage rules." This removes a major investor concern upfront and shows you've thought through the compliance landscape

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers are internally consistent across the slides, so you don't have a mathematical contradiction problem. However, you're missing a critical bridge: the AED 800K revenue claim needs a clear breakdown showing how it maps to your six revenue streams (commission share, developer partnerships, ancillary services, SaaS licensing, brokerage commission, and advisory services). Right now investors will wonder which streams generated that 800K and whether it's repeatable—add a simple one-line callout like "AED 800K comprised of: AED 450K developer partnerships + AED 250K commission share + AED 100K ancillary services" to make the revenue feel real and traceable

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service... This automation and intelligence is intended to disrupt local and global real estate markets by delivering scalable, data-driven services that traditional agents cannot match

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo, hllooerggeyyin AA, inns nninuuteaanll dRReeedpp sooolrrettly
findings.suggestion: You've got corrupted text scattered throughout your footer — that garbled string "TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo..." appears on nearly every slide and immediately signals a file corruption or encoding issue to investors. Find and replace that entire footer section with a clean, single-line disclaimer like "This document is confidential and for authorized recipients only" — it takes 30 seconds and removes a major credibility hit that makes your otherwise solid traction numbers look sloppy.
